topic: Hana's endgame improving — she slowed down

turns.content: Good afternoon. I wanted to share something that pleased me today. One of my go students, Hana, played an endgame sequence this morning that was genuinely impressive. She has been struggling with the endgame for months, and today something changed. I think she finally slowed down.

Good afternoon! That's wonderful to hear. The endgame is where patience really shows — or the lack of it. When you say she slowed down, do you mean she was taking more time to read out the sequences before playing, rather than making quick intuitive moves?

Exactly. In her previous games, she would reach the endgame with a comfortable advantage and then rush through the final fifty or sixty moves as though the outcome were already decided. She would lose two, sometimes three points through careless boundary plays. Today she paused before each move. She counted. She compared the value of one move against another, and she chose correctly almost every time. She said she had been reviewing professional endgame sequences on her own, and she realized that the players she admired most spent as much time on the final thirty moves as on the opening. I think that observation reframed the endgame for her — not as a formality, but as a third act with its own logic.

That's a really mature insight for a student to arrive at on her own. The third act analogy is exactly right — the endgame isn't just wrapping up; it's where precision determines whether the rest of the game's work holds or collapses. And the fact that she came to this understanding through her own study, rather than you telling her, is especially significant. She owns the insight now.

Did you notice her counting technique specifically — was she doing miai counting, or more of an intuitive comparison?

She was using a simplified form of miai counting. Not perfectly — she rounded when she should have been precise, and she miscalculated a reverse sente sequence by half a point. But the framework was there. I think precision will come with practice. The important thing is that she now understands what she is trying to calculate. In mathematics, understanding the question is more than half the work. The computation follows.

That's such a good point — if a student doesn't understand what miai counting is actually measuring, no amount of practice will help. But once the concept clicks, the arithmetic is just arithmetic. And a half-point error on a reverse sente is very forgivable — those are genuinely tricky to evaluate. The fact that she recognized it as reverse sente at all puts her well ahead of most players at her level.

Are you planning to build on this in upcoming lessons?

Yes. I will design a series of endgame-only problems for her — positions where the middle game is already resolved and the entire exercise is to find the optimal sequence. I want to isolate the variable, as it were. If she practices counting in a controlled environment, without the emotional investment of a full game, the skill will solidify more quickly. Teaching is a kind of experimental design — one must vary the conditions systematically.

Isolating the variable is a perfect approach. You could also vary the difficulty — some positions where the game is within two or three points, so each move genuinely matters, and others where the lead is large enough that she can practice technique without pressure. The method stays the same regardless of the margin, and that contrast would teach her something important about discipline.

That is a good suggestion. I will include both types. I am very pleased with her progress. There is a particular satisfaction in watching a student overcome a weakness through their own effort. I provided the structure, but the understanding was hers. I think that is the best outcome a teacher can hope for — to make oneself unnecessary. Thank you for listening. Good afternoon.

Making oneself unnecessary — that might be the most elegant definition of good teaching I've heard. You built the framework, she found her own way through it. That's exactly how lasting learning happens.

Good afternoon. Enjoy designing those endgame problems — I suspect Hana will rise to them.
